answers: Mouldy groundnuts contain aflatoxin, a poison that can damage the liver and cause cancer over time. Never eat groundnuts, kuli kuli, or groundnut paste that taste bitter, look discoloured, or are mouldy — throw them away.
Aflatoxin from mouldy groundnuts and maize is one of Ghana's biggest hidden food dangers. Buy fresh, dry groundnuts, store them somewhere dry, and discard any that are shrivelled or bitter.

Cooked rice left at room temperature grows Bacillus cereus, which causes vomiting and diarrhoea — 'fried rice syndrome'. Refrigerate leftover rice or jollof within 1-2 hours and reheat it until steaming hot, only once.
Never leave cooked rice out overnight in Ghana's heat. If jollof or plain rice sat out for hours, throw it away — reheating won't destroy the toxins the bacteria produce.
A swollen, bloated, or leaking tin can mean botulism — a rare but deadly poison. Never taste or eat food from a bulging or badly dented can. Discard it without opening.
If canned tomato, sardines, or corned beef has a bulging lid, hisses strongly, or smells off, do not eat it. When in doubt, throw it out.
Do not just cut off the mouldy part of bread — mould roots spread through soft foods you cannot see. Throw away the whole loaf. Store bread in a cool dry place and buy smaller amounts more often.

Waakye is safe when freshly cooked and kept hot or refrigerated. The stew and shito are the parts that spoil fastest — if they taste sour or the waakye sat out for hours, don't eat it. Reheat thoroughly.
Buy waakye from a busy vendor whose food is fresh. Waakye leaves used for colour are traditional and fine; the risk is stew and protein left warm for too long.
Kenkey is fermented, which helps preserve it, but it can still grow mould in humidity. Check the surface — white mould specks mean throw it away. Keep kenkey wrapped and eat within a day or two.
Freshly made kenkey lasts a couple of days at room temperature because of fermentation, but always check for sour smell beyond normal or fuzzy mould before eating.

Banku is fermented corn and cassava dough and is safe eaten fresh and hot. Leftover banku should be refrigerated and reheated well. The okro or pepper soup served with it spoils faster than the banku itself.
Fufu should be eaten fresh. The soup (light soup, groundnut soup, palm nut soup) is what spoils quickly in the heat — refrigerate leftover soup within 2 hours and boil it again before serving.
Jollof is safe freshly cooked. Because it's rice, it grows bacteria fast if left out — refrigerate leftovers within 1-2 hours and reheat until piping hot, once only.
Kelewele is safest bought hot and fresh from a busy vendor. Avoid pieces fried in very dark, reused oil — it can taste bitter and be harmful. Eat it the same day.

Kontomire and other leafy greens can carry bacteria and parasites from soil and irrigation water. Wash each leaf under clean running water and cook it thoroughly — never eat it raw or lightly steamed.
Beans and red red are nutritious and safe when well cooked. Undercooked beans can upset the stomach. Leftover bean stew should be refrigerated and reheated to a rolling boil.

Well-made shito is fried in a lot of oil, which helps it keep for weeks in a clean, dry jar. Always use a clean dry spoon — water or a wet spoon introduces mould. If it smells fermented or grows mould, discard it.

Smoked and salted fish like koobi and momone keep well only when fully dry. Once moisture gets in, mould and maggots appear fast. Store them dry and airtight; discard any that are damp, slimy, or smell of ammonia.
Buy smoked fish that is hard and dry, not soft or moist. Sun the fish to keep it dry during rainy season, and throw away any with mould or a rotten smell.

Fresh fish has clear bright eyes, red gills, firm flesh, and a clean sea smell. Dull sunken eyes, grey gills, mushy flesh, or a strong rotten odour mean it's spoiled. Cook fresh fish the same day or freeze it.
Wagashi (local soft cheese) spoils quickly because it is fresh and moist. Keep it cool in clean water or refrigerated, change the water, and fry or cook it soon after buying.
Cook eggs until both white and yolk are firm to avoid salmonella. Avoid raw or runny eggs, and never buy cracked eggs. Store eggs in a cool place and wash your hands after handling the shells.
Boil fresh or raw milk before drinking to kill bacteria. Once opened, tinned or reconstituted powdered milk must be refrigerated and used within a day — it spoils fast in the heat.
Honey keeps for a very long time and rarely spoils. However, never give honey to a baby under 1 year — it can cause infant botulism. Cloudy or fermented-smelling honey with bubbles has gone off.

Fresh palm oil is bright orange-red and rich in vitamins A and E. Store it sealed away from light and heat. Repeatedly reheated palm oil turns rancid and harmful — discard oil that is very dark, foamy, or smells burnt.
Cooking oil reused many times becomes dark, foamy, and rancid, producing compounds linked to inflammation and disease. Change frying oil regularly and never buy street food fried in near-black oil.

Seasoning cubes are safe in normal amounts but are very high in salt. Using too many raises your salt intake and blood pressure. Use one cube where possible and add flavour with fresh onion, ginger, garlic, and pepper instead.

Too much salt raises blood pressure, a major cause of stroke and heart disease in Ghana. Cut back on seasoning cubes, table salt, and salted fish. Aim for less than one teaspoon of salt total per day.

Sugary soft drinks and too much sugar raise the risk of diabetes and tooth decay. Choose water over minerals and sweet drinks, and limit sugar in tea and porridge. Read labels — many 'juice' drinks are mostly sugar.

To manage high blood pressure: reduce salt and seasoning cubes, eat more fruits and vegetables, stay active, avoid excess alcohol, and take your prescribed medicine every day even when you feel fine. Check your BP regularly.
High blood pressure often has no symptoms but can cause stroke. Never stop your BP medicine because you feel well — that is when it is working. Cut salt and see your doctor for regular checks.

If you have diabetes, control your blood sugar by limiting sugary foods and drinks, eating smaller portions of banku, rice, and fufu, staying active, and taking medicine as prescribed. Never stop treatment without your doctor's advice.

Diabetes needs steady management, not herbal 'cures'. Eat more vegetables and protein, reduce sugar and refined carbs, and monitor your sugar. Untreated diabetes damages the eyes, kidneys, and nerves.

For ulcers and heartburn, avoid very spicy or acidic food, alcohol, and painkillers like ibuprofen and aspirin on an empty stomach. Eat regular small meals. If you have burning pain, black stools, or vomiting blood, see a doctor promptly.

For constipation, drink more water and eat high-fibre foods like fruits, vegetables, beans, and whole grains. Stay active. If it lasts more than a week or comes with pain or blood, see a health worker.

Low blood (anaemia) is common, especially in pregnancy. Eat iron-rich foods like kontomire, liver, beans, and fish, plus vitamin C fruits to absorb iron. Take iron and folic acid if prescribed, and get tested if you feel very weak or dizzy.

Cholera causes sudden heavy watery diarrhoea and can kill quickly through dehydration. Start ORS immediately and go to a clinic fast. Prevent it by drinking safe water, washing hands, and avoiding food from unhygienic sources during an outbreak.

Typhoid spreads through contaminated food and water and causes prolonged fever, headache, and stomach pain. It needs proper antibiotics from a doctor — do not self-treat. Prevent it with safe water, good hand hygiene, and clean food.

Treat malaria with WHO-approved ACT (artemisinin combination therapy) such as artemether-lumefantrine, ideally after a positive test. Finish the full course. Seek urgent care for a child or anyone with confusion, convulsions, or unable to drink.
Do not treat malaria with chloroquine alone — resistance is widespread. Get tested, use an approved ACT, and sleep under a treated net to prevent it.

ORS (oral rehydration salts) replaces fluids lost in diarrhoea and vomiting and saves lives. Mix one sachet in the stated amount of clean water and sip often. For children with diarrhoea, add zinc for 10-14 days. Seek care for sunken eyes or no urine.
You can make an emergency rehydration drink with six level teaspoons of sugar and half a teaspoon of salt in one litre of clean, boiled water — but sachet ORS is safer and preferred.

Paracetamol relieves fever and mild pain. Adults take 500mg-1g every 4-6 hours, up to 4g (usually 8 tablets) in 24 hours. More than that can badly damage the liver, so never exceed the dose even if pain continues.
Paracetamol is safe at the right dose and is preferred for fever and pain if you have an ulcer or stomach problems. Check other medicines too — many cold remedies already contain paracetamol.

Ibuprofen and diclofenac ease pain and swelling but can irritate the stomach and harm the kidneys. Take them with food, avoid them if you have an ulcer, and don't use them long-term without a doctor's advice.
Low-dose aspirin is used to protect the heart in some patients, but only on a doctor's advice. Regular aspirin can irritate the stomach and cause bleeding — do not give it to children with fever.

Antibiotics only work on bacterial infections, not colds or viruses. Take them only when prescribed and finish the full course even if you feel better — stopping early breeds resistant bacteria that are much harder to treat.
Never buy antibiotics from roadside sellers or share them. Misusing antibiotics is fuelling drug resistance in Ghana. Use them exactly as a doctor or pharmacist directs.

Tramadol is a strong prescription painkiller that is dangerously misused. Taking high doses to get 'high' or for energy can cause seizures, breathing failure, addiction, and death. Only use tramadol prescribed by a doctor, at the exact dose.
Tramadol bought on the street is often counterfeit or extremely high-dose. Abusing it is destroying lives — seek help from a health worker if you or someone you know is dependent on it.

Codeine cough syrups are misused to get high, which is dangerous and addictive. Use cough medicine only as directed. Persistent cough for more than two weeks needs a doctor to rule out TB or other illness.

Do not take expired medicines. They can lose potency — dangerous for antibiotics and heart or BP drugs — and some break down into harmful substances. Check the expiry date every time and dispose of old medicines safely.

Store medicines in a cool, dry place below 25°C, out of sunlight, in their original packaging. Ghana's heat degrades drugs fast. Never keep tablets in the bathroom — humidity ruins them. Some syrups need the fridge after opening; check the label.
Keep all medicines out of reach of children, ideally in a locked box. If a tablet or syrup changes colour, smell, or texture, do not use it.

Counterfeit medicines may contain no active ingredient, the wrong one, or poison. Buy only from licensed pharmacies, keep the receipt, and scan the drug's QR code in FoodTrace to verify the batch. Report suspected fakes to FDA Ghana.
Warning signs of fake drugs: spelling errors on the pack, unusual colour or smell, missing batch number or expiry, and prices too good to be true. When unsure, don't take it — verify first.
Medicines sold on buses, streets, and open markets are unregulated, often fake, and stored in damaging heat. Never buy medicine from hawkers. Use a licensed pharmacy and verify with FoodTrace.

Prescription medicines — especially antibiotics, BP, diabetes, and mental health drugs — should only be taken under a doctor's or pharmacist's guidance. Self-medicating can hide serious illness and cause harm. When in doubt, get checked.

Some herbal remedies help, but many are unregulated and can damage the liver or interact with your medicines. Look for FDA Ghana approval on the pack, tell your doctor what herbs you take, and never replace proven treatment for serious illness with unverified cures.

Moringa, ginger, and hibiscus (sobolo) in moderate amounts are generally fine. Be very cautious with strong 'bitters' and cure-all mixtures — dose and ingredients are unpredictable in unlicensed products.

Many skin-lightening creams contain mercury, steroids, or high hydroquinone that damage the skin and kidneys and can cause permanent harm. FDA Ghana bans these. Avoid bleaching products and protect your natural skin instead.

Locally distilled spirits like akpeteshie vary greatly in strength and can be contaminated with methanol, which causes blindness or death. Drink only regulated, labelled alcohol in moderation, and never on an empty stomach or with medicines.

In pregnancy, eat well-cooked food, wash fruits and vegetables, take folic acid and iron as prescribed, and avoid alcohol and unprescribed medicines and herbs. Attend antenatal clinic and report any fever, bleeding, or swelling promptly.
Pregnant women should avoid raw or undercooked meat and eggs, unpasteurised milk, and too much salt. Always check with your midwife or doctor before taking any medicine.

Breast milk is the safest, complete food for babies under 6 months — no water needed. If you must express milk, store it in a clean covered container: a few hours at room temperature, up to a day in the fridge. Always check it smells fresh.
Exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months protects babies from diarrhoea and infection. After 6 months, add clean, freshly prepared complementary foods while continuing to breastfeed.
Baby foods like koko and Tom Brown must be freshly made and fed immediately — never store and reheat warm porridge, as bacteria multiply fast. Sterilise or thoroughly wash all feeding utensils.
Mix infant formula with boiled, cooled water, feed within an hour, and discard leftovers. Introduce weaning foods one at a time; if diarrhoea follows a new food, stop it and see a health worker.

Intestinal worms are common in children. Deworm every 6 months with an approved medicine (like albendazole) from a pharmacy or health centre, wash hands, wear footwear, and keep food covered from flies.

Washing hands with soap and clean water before eating and cooking, and after the toilet, prevents most food-borne illness. When water is scarce, use hand sanitizer. Food vendors should never handle money and food with the same bare hands.
Flies carry bacteria from waste onto food and cause diarrhoea and typhoid. Always keep food covered, dispose of rubbish properly, and avoid buying food displayed uncovered with flies around it.

Street food can be safe if freshly cooked and kept hot or covered. Choose busy vendors whose food turns over fast, avoid pre-cooked food sitting out for hours, and skip anything that smells or tastes off.
A good food vendor keeps the area clean, covers the food, and serves with a spoon or gloves. If the stew is lukewarm or the oil looks black, buy elsewhere.

Cooked food left at room temperature for more than 2 hours (1 hour in strong heat) should be thrown away. To keep leftovers, refrigerate quickly and reheat until steaming hot all the way through, only once.
Do not repeatedly reheat the same pot of soup or stew over several days. Portion leftovers, refrigerate promptly, and reheat only what you'll eat.

Without a fridge, cook only what you'll eat, keep soups on the boil and reheat them twice a day, and use dried, salted, or smoked foods that keep longer. During power cuts, keep the freezer closed and use thawing food quickly.
Keep your fridge cold (below 5°C) and freezer frozen. Store raw meat and fish on the lowest shelf so juices don't drip onto other food, cover everything, and don't overfill so cold air can circulate.

Thaw frozen chicken or meat in the fridge or under cold running water, never on the counter in the heat. Once thawed, cook it fully the same day and never refreeze raw thawed meat. Buy frozen items that are still solidly frozen.
Cook meat until the juices run clear with no pink inside to kill harmful bacteria. Keep raw meat away from ready-to-eat food, and wash hands, knives, and boards after handling it.

Raw chicken often carries salmonella. Never leave it at room temperature for more than an hour in Ghana's heat, cook it thoroughly, and wash everything that touched it. Undercooked chicken is a common cause of food poisoning.
Wash all fruits and vegetables under clean running water before eating or cooking, even those you peel. A 10-minute soak in mild salt water helps remove surface dirt and residues. Peel where you can.

Pesticide residues on produce are a real concern. Washing well and peeling reduces exposure but doesn't remove everything. Buy from trusted or certified sources, and use FoodTrace to check packaged produce and farm inputs where available.

For suspected food poisoning, sip ORS or clean water often to prevent dehydration and rest. Go to a clinic urgently if there's high fever, bloody stool, severe pain, or you can't keep fluids down, or for young children and the elderly.
Most mild food poisoning settles in a day or two with fluids and rest. Note what you ate — if it was from a vendor, report it so others can be protected.

Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, little or dark urine, sunken eyes, and weakness. Drink ORS or clean water steadily. Severe dehydration, especially in children or the elderly, is an emergency — go to a health facility.

If you're unsure water is safe, boil it for at least one minute and let it cool, or use a certified filter. Always use safe water for drinking, brushing teeth, washing produce, and mixing baby formula.
During any cholera or flooding alert, treat all drinking water even if it looks clear — boil it or add the recommended treatment.

Sachet 'pure water' from registered brands is generally safe. Avoid sachets that are torn, cloudy, swollen, or have no clear brand and FDA details — they may be untreated or counterfeit. Rinse the sachet before biting it open.
Borehole and well water can be contaminated with germs or chemicals. Have it tested if you drink it regularly, and boil or filter it to be safe. Never drink untreated stream or river water.

Cosmetics are regulated by FDA Ghana too. Check for FDA approval, avoid products with mercury or strong steroids, and don't use creams and makeup past their expiry — they can cause skin reactions and infection.
Heat kills most harmful germs. Cook meat, poultry, and fish thoroughly, and when reheating, make food steaming hot all the way through — not just warm on the outside, where bacteria survive.
Store gari, rice, flour, and beans in sealed containers in a cool dry place to keep out moisture and weevils. Discard any that smell musty, clump with mould, or are heavily infested with insects.

Check tinned foods for a firm, undamaged can and a valid expiry date. Once opened, move leftovers to a covered container and refrigerate — don't store food in the open tin. Discard any can that is bulging or leaking.

Throw away food that is mouldy, slimy, discoloured, or smells sour or 'off'. Some spoilage produces toxins that cooking won't destroy. When in doubt, throw it out — it's cheaper than a hospital visit.
Never eat food or take medicine past its expiry date, even if it looks fine — some dangers are invisible. In FoodTrace, scan the QR code to see the registered expiry for that exact batch.
'Best before' is about quality; 'use by' and medicine expiry are about safety. In Ghana's heat, treat all dates strictly and store items properly so they last.

If a product is recalled, stop using it at once, don't pass it to anyone, and return or safely dispose of it. In FoodTrace a red RECALLED screen means do not use it. If you already consumed it and feel unwell, seek care.
Recalls are issued when a product fails safety checks. Scanning in FoodTrace instantly shows if a batch has an active recall — always check before consuming.

To scan: open FoodTrace, tap the Scan tab, and point your camera at the product's QR code. You'll see green (safe), yellow (caution), or red (recalled) with the full details and a spoken summary in English or Twi.
If the camera can't read the code, type the batch code manually in the scanner. The result comes from the registered product database.

To report a suspicious or unsafe product, use the Report option in FoodTrace with a short description and, if you can, a photo and location. Regulators review reports and can issue recalls that warn everyone.

In the FoodTrace marketplace, verified sellers post products that show a live safety badge (FDA Approved, EPA Cleared, or Recalled). You can like, comment, share to WhatsApp, and scan any post to verify it before buying.
Every marketplace post is reviewed by a regulator before it goes public, and its badge comes straight from the safety system — so a seller can't fake a 'verified' label.
FoodTrace reads your scan result aloud and supports English and Twi. Switch the language on the scan or account screen, then scan again to hear the safety summary in your preferred language.

FDA Ghana regulates food, medicines, cosmetics, and devices — approving products, inspecting them, and issuing recalls. Look for the FDA approval mark, and use FoodTrace to check a product's live status before buying.
An FDA-approved product has passed safety and quality checks. Products with no FDA registration may be counterfeit or illegally imported without any testing.
The EPA regulates pesticides and farm chemicals in Ghana. Farmers should use only approved inputs at the correct dose and respect the withdrawal period before harvest, so produce is safe to eat.

Vitamins help when your diet lacks them, but more is not better — high doses of some vitamins are harmful. Prefer a varied diet of fruits, vegetables, and protein, and take supplements a health worker recommends.
Be cautious with 'blood tonics', appetite or weight-gain pills, and energy boosters sold informally — many contain steroids or unlisted drugs that harm the body. Get proper advice before taking them.

Energy drinks are high in caffeine and sugar. Too many can cause a racing heart, poor sleep, and anxiety, and they're risky mixed with alcohol. Have them rarely and never rely on them to stay awake for long periods.

If someone is choking and cannot breathe or speak, give firm back blows between the shoulder blades and abdominal thrusts, and call for help. For a baby, use gentle back blows supporting the head. Seek medical help urgently.

For a minor burn, cool it under clean running water for about 20 minutes, don't apply oil, toothpaste, or ice, and cover loosely with a clean cloth. Get medical care for large, deep, or blistering burns.

For a snakebite, keep the person calm and still, keep the bitten limb below heart level, remove tight items, and get to a hospital fast for antivenom. Don't cut the wound, suck it, or apply a tight tourniquet.
Whoever cooks should keep clean: wash hands often, tie back hair, keep surfaces and boards clean, separate raw and cooked food, and stay away from cooking when sick with diarrhoea or vomiting.

Don't use the same knife or board for raw meat and ready-to-eat food without washing them, and never let raw meat juices touch cooked food. Cross-contamination is a common, hidden cause of food poisoning.

A child with diarrhoea needs ORS after every loose stool plus zinc for 10-14 days, and continued feeding and breastfeeding. Go to a clinic urgently if there's blood, persistent vomiting, sunken eyes, or the child is very weak.
Keep taking your blood pressure medicine every day, even when you feel well — feeling fine means it's working. Stopping suddenly can cause a dangerous spike. Discuss any changes with your doctor.

I'm your FoodTrace GH safety assistant. Ask me about Ghanaian foods and how to store them, food poisoning, clean water, medicines and dosing, malaria, counterfeit drugs, pregnancy and baby feeding, and how to use the app to scan, report, and shop safely.
You're welcome! Stay safe, and scan before you eat or take any medicine. Ask me anything else anytime.
Anytime! Remember, when in doubt, scan it or throw it out. I'm here whenever you need help.
